metadata.summary: Go layout: cmd/ entry points, internal/ private code, pkg/ public API only. Hand-wire dependencies in main.go, use .golangci.yml, Makefile, multi-stage Dockerfile with CGO_ENABLED=0.

content.title: Go Project Structure
content.summary: Standard Go project layout following community conventions: cmd/ for entry points, internal/ for private app code, pkg/ only for truly public API. Dependencies wired by hand in cmd/.../main.go; no DI framework until hand-wiring exceeds ~100 lines. Core rules: internal/ default, CGO_ENABLED=0 in containers, one .golangci.yml shared by local dev and CI.

why.p: Go's toolchain enforces internal/ boundaries, preventing accidental coupling across packages. Starting flat and promoting to structure only when justified avoids premature abstraction. Cyclic imports — Go's most common agent mistake — are caught at compile time; extracting a shared types package into internal/model resolves them without exposing symbols unnecessarily.

list.item: Bootstrapping a new Go service, CLI, or library
Refactoring a flat single-package program that has crossed ~3 entry points or ~5 domain concepts
Standardizing across multiple Go services so agents can navigate predictably
Wiring DI manually with constructors in cmd/<bin>/main.go
Producing repeatable Docker + Makefile + module hygiene that LLMs can replicate
One-file scripts and learning exercises — flat layout beats premature internal/cmd/pkg
Pure libraries: don't add internal/ and cmd/ if all you ship is exported packages
Code generators that already define their own layout (buf, kubebuilder, cobra-cli)
Multi-language monorepos where Go is one of N — use the org-wide layout, not Go-specific defaults
When team consensus differs: enforcing golang-standards/project-layout against the team will lose

section.name: Directory Layout

rule.statement: Use internal/ as the default for all application code; add pkg/ only when an external module explicitly imports it.
rule.rationale: Go's toolchain enforces internal/ boundaries — external packages cannot import it. pkg/ without a clear "external consumers" rule becomes a dumping ground.

section.p: Standard tree: cmd/api/main.go + cmd/worker/main.go. internal/config/, internal/database/, internal/handler/, internal/middleware/, internal/model/, internal/repository/, internal/service/. pkg/ (public library only). api/ (OpenAPI spec). scripts/, deployments/ (Dockerfile, k8s). go.mod, go.sum, Makefile.

section.name: Where It Fails / Limitations
section.p: The popular golang-standards/project-layout is community-curated, not official; agents quote it as canon and overbuild.
pkg/ is often misused as a dumping ground; without a clear "this is API for outsiders" rule, it loses meaning.
internal/ boundary is enforced by the toolchain but agents still leak through interfaces with public types.
Cyclic-import errors hit late and confuse agents — they "fix" by exporting more symbols, worsening coupling.
DI by hand-wiring in main.go grows long; agents reach for wire/fx too early.
Default cmd/<name>/main.go layout creates ceremony for tiny CLIs that fit in one file.

rule.statement: Keep cmd/<bin>/main.go thin: parse flags, load config, wire deps, call Run(ctx); all logic lives in internal packages.
rule.rationale: A fat main.go is untestable. Thin main with explicit wiring makes every dependency visible, swap-able in tests, and documented by its constructor call.

rule.statement: Load all configuration from environment variables in a Config struct at startup; use os.Getenv with explicit defaults, not dotenv in production.
rule.rationale: dotenv overrides env vars set by the orchestrator. In containerised deployments, env vars come from the platform secrets layer; dotenv belongs in local dev only.

rule.statement: Introduce wire or fx only when hand-wiring in main.go exceeds ~100 lines; resist adding it for small services.
rule.rationale: DI frameworks add a compile step and learning overhead. For fewer than 10 constructors, explicit wiring in main.go is clearer and easier to trace.

rule.statement: Commit a Makefile with standard targets: build, run, test, test-coverage, lint, fmt, tidy, clean, docker-build.
rule.rationale: A consistent Makefile means any developer or agent can get started without reading docs. LLMs can reliably invoke make targets without guessing tool flags.

rule.statement: Use a multi-stage Dockerfile: Go builder stage with CGO_ENABLED=0, then a minimal alpine runtime. Set USER nobody:nobody in the runtime stage.
rule.rationale: CGO_ENABLED=0 produces a fully static binary that runs in a scratch or alpine image with no libc dependency. Running as nobody reduces attack surface in container escapes.

rule.statement: Commit a .golangci.yml to the repo and run identical rules locally and in CI; include contextcheck linter to catch missing context.Context plumbing.
rule.rationale: Without a committed config, local and CI linting diverge. contextcheck catches the common agent mistake of omitting ctx parameters on repository methods.

rule.statement: Thread context.Context as the first argument through every repository method and service call; never use context.Background() inside a handler.
rule.rationale: Without ctx plumbing, request cancellation and deadline propagation don't reach the DB layer — a cancelled client request still consumes DB connections.

antipattern.description: Cyclic imports: package A imports B, package B imports A.
antipattern.reason: Go toolchain rejects cyclic imports at compile time. The fix is to extract shared types into a third package (e.g., internal/model), not to export more symbols upward.

antipattern.description: Global state: var DB *sql.DB at package level.
antipattern.reason: Global DB makes parallel tests interfere, prevents multiple instances, and hides dependencies. Use constructor injection: NewUserRepository(db *sql.DB).

antipattern.description: Everything in the main package: type User, type Order, func CreateUser, func HandleRequest all in package main.
antipattern.reason: Single-package projects become untestable and unmaintainable above ~500 lines. Separate by responsibility into internal/ sub-packages.

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: For a new project, run a scaffolder agent that asks for module path, binary names, and DB driver, then emits the cmd/, internal/, pkg/, Makefile, Dockerfile, and a single test. For an existing project, an auditor agent maps imports, finds cycles and internal/ violations, and proposes a single refactor PR. Treat go vet, staticcheck, and golangci-lint as the quality gate the agent must pass before commit.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — design + scaffold + verify with quality gates
A custom go-scaffolder (haiku) — emits the canonical tree from a small JSON spec
A custom go-import-auditor (sonnet) — runs go list -deps, detects cycles and internal/ leaks, drafts a refactor plan
Keep cmd/<bin>/main.go thin: parse flags, load config, wire deps, call Run(ctx) from an internal package
Put domain types in internal/model; keep them DB-tag-free if you also expose them via JSON — use separate DTOs in internal/handler
Use internal/ aggressively; reach for pkg/ only when an external module imports it
Define Repository and Service interfaces near consumers, not producers — keeps wiring testable and avoids leaky cross-package types
One linter config: commit .golangci.yml and run identical rules locally and in CI
Pin Go version in go.mod (go 1.22) and Dockerfile together; CI must match
Always set CGO_ENABLED=0 in container builds unless you need cgo; saves base image size and runtime headaches

section.name: AI-Agent Gotchas
section.p: Agents over-apply the golang-standards/project-layout to tiny projects; require a size threshold before adopting.
LLMs put everything in pkg/ because tutorials show it; reviewers must enforce internal/ default.
Cyclic imports are a frequent agent mistake — they "fix" by moving symbols up; instead, extract a third package owning the shared types.
Agents add wire/fx immediately; require a "manual wire grew past 100 lines" trigger before adding.
Agents forget context.Context plumbing through repository methods; lint with contextcheck linter.
Agents leave global var DB *sql.DB; require a constructor pattern (NewUserRepository(db)).
Human-in-loop checkpoint: any DB schema change colocated under internal/database/migrations/ needs a separate PR and review.

template.purpose: Bash script to materialise standard tree from module path + binary names
Build, test, lint, coverage, Docker targets
